%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 1.2-1 Time Shifting

> **Section Objective:** Learn how replacing \(t\) by \(t - T\) moves a signal in time without changing its shape.

## Concepts In This Section

- Time delay
- Time advance
- Sign rule for \(x(t - T)\)
- Shifted support boundary
- Exponential example

## 1. The time-shift rule

If the same event that occurs at time \(t\) in \(x(t)\) occurs \(T\) seconds later in \(\phi(t)\), then the two signals must agree when the delayed clock catches up. This equation compares the same waveform event at two different clock readings: \(t\) in the original and \(t + T\) in the shifted version.

$$\phi(t+T) = x(t)$$

## 1. The time-shift rule

This is the core time-shifting formula.

- \(x(t)\) — the original signal
- \(\phi(t)\) — the shifted signal
- \(T\) — the shift amount in seconds
- \(t\) — the time variable

**Exam trigger:** Whenever the argument is \(t - T\) with \(T > 0\), the waveform shifts **right** by \(T\) seconds (a delay).

**Most common misuse:** Students see the minus sign and shift left — but \(x(t - T)\) moves the graph to the **right**. The minus means the event arrives later.

**Minimal example:** \(x(t - 3)\) is \(x(t)\) delayed by 3 seconds; every feature appears 3 seconds later on the time axis.

$$\phi(t) = x(t - T)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. 1.4](/figures/page-071-unknown-1.png)
*Three stacked plots showing the original signal \(x(t)\), the delayed signal \(\phi(t) = x(t - T)\) shifted right by \(T\), and the advanced signal \(x(t + T)\) shifted left by \(T\).*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure contains three time-domain panels sharing a horizontal \(t\)-axis with a vertical reference at \(t = 0\). Panel (a) shows the original waveform \(x(t)\). Panel (b) shows \(\phi(t) = x(t - T)\): the identical waveform displaced to the right by \(T\) seconds, with dotted vertical lines and an arrow labeled \(T\) marking the displacement. Panel (c) shows \(x(t + T)\): the same waveform displaced to the left by \(T\) seconds. Students should notice that only the horizontal position changes — the shape and amplitude are completely preserved in both the delayed and advanced versions.</div>

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="exam_pattern_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. Watch the sign rule move the graph

This is the original signal from Example 1.3. The unit step \(u(t)\) acts as an on/off switch: it equals 1 for \(t \geq 0\) and 0 for \(t < 0\). As a result, the exponential \(e^{-2t}\) is active only for \(t \geq 0\) and the signal is identically zero before \(t = 0\). The goal is to move this start time by applying a time shift.

$$x(t) = e^{-2t}\,u(t)$$

## 3. Representative example: delaying an exponential

Replacing every \(t\) with \(t - 1\) delays the entire signal by 1 second.

- \(u(t - 1)\) turns on when \(t - 1 \geq 0\), i.e., at \(t = 1\).
- The signal is **zero for \(t < 1\)** and equals \(e^{-2(t-1)}\) **for \(t \geq 1\)**.
- The exponential still starts at value 1 (since \(e^{-2(0)} = 1\)), but that starting value now occurs at \(t = 1\) instead of \(t = 0\).

#### COMMON TRAP
Writing \(e^{-2t}\,u(t-1)\) is **wrong**. It shifts the step boundary to \(t = 1\) but leaves the exponential's time origin at \(t = 0\), producing a mismatched signal. Both the step and the exponent argument must use \(t - 1\).

$$x_d(t) = x(t-1) = e^{-2(t-1)}\,u(t-1)$$

## 3. Representative example: delaying an exponential

Writing \(x(t + 1)\) is the same as \(x(t - (-1))\), so this is an **advance by 1 second** — the waveform moves to the left.

- \(u(t + 1)\) turns on when \(t + 1 \geq 0\), i.e., at \(t = -1\).
- The signal starts at \(t = -1\) and follows \(e^{-2(t+1)}\) for \(t \geq -1\).

**Quick check:** \(x(t + 2)\) means an advance by 2 seconds (start at \(t = -2\)), **not** a delay by 2 seconds. A plus inside the argument always means the event happens earlier.

$$x_a(t) = x(t+1) = e^{-2(t+1)}\,u(t+1)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. 1.5](/figures/page-072-fig__1_5-1.png)
*Three plots showing \(x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)\) in (a), its 1-second delay \(x(t-1) = e^{-2(t-1)}u(t-1)\) in (b), and its 1-second advance \(x(t+1) = e^{-2(t+1)}u(t+1)\) in (c).*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure contains three time-domain panels with a horizontal \(t\)-axis. Panel (a) shows \(x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)\): the signal jumps to 1 at \(t = 0\) and decays exponentially, remaining zero for \(t &lt; 0\). Panel (b) shows the delayed version: the jump and decay begin at \(t = 1\), with the signal zero for \(t &lt; 1\). Panel (c) shows the advanced version: the jump and decay begin at \(t = -1\), with the signal zero for \(t &lt; -1\). Students should notice that the exponential shape and the initial amplitude of 1 are preserved in all three panels — only the start time shifts.</div>

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- If \(\phi(t+T) = x(t)\), then \(\phi(t) = x(t-T)\): the delayed signal is found by replacing \(t\) with \(t - T\).
- Direction rule: positive \(T\) in \(x(t-T)\) shifts the graph **right** (delay); a plus inside, as in \(x(t+T)\), shifts **left** (advance).
- Delay example: \(x_d(t) = x(t-1) = e^{-2(t-1)}u(t-1)\) starts at \(t = 1\); both the exponent and step must use \(t-1\).
- Advance example: \(x_a(t) = x(t+1) = e^{-2(t+1)}u(t+1)\) starts at \(t = -1\); shape and amplitude are unchanged.

*Next, we will use similar thinking to understand time scaling.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
